title: Adirondack Stool / End Table
summary: The little table every Adirondack chair is missing — a slat-top stool from three boards, pocket-holed so no fastener shows on the top.
description:
Build a pair of Adirondack chairs and you'll discover the missing piece within one iced tea: there's nowhere to put anything down. This stool is the answer — a 14-1/2" slat-top square that works as an end table between chairs, a footrest, or extra seating when the patio overflows.

It's also a tidy first pocket-hole project. Every joint is a 3/4" pocket hole with 1-1/4" screws, all hidden on the insides, so the finished piece shows clean faces everywhere. The frame is 2x2 legs with 1x3 aprons; the lower side supports and stretcher stiffen it enough to stand on.

Match the finish to your chairs — same stain or paint, and the set reads as bought-together. If it lives outside, use exterior glue and finish; the lumber list is cheap enough that weatherproofing is the only line item worth fussing over.

tools.note: Every joint — 3/4" setting, 1-1/4" screws, holes on the insides.
Handy for tacking the top slats while glue sets; screws from below work too.

steps.title: Build the two leg sets
steps.body: Drill 3/4"-setting pocket holes in the ends of two aprons and join each to a pair of legs with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue. Two identical leg sets, pocket holes facing where the inside of the stool will be.

steps.title: Join the leg sets
steps.body: Pocket-hole the remaining two aprons and connect the leg sets into a square frame, holes to the insides. Check the top is square — the slats will broadcast any parallelogram.

steps.title: Add the side supports
steps.body: Mark 3-1/2" up from the bottom of each leg and attach the two 1x2 side supports at the marks, centered on the legs, with pocket screws and glue.

steps.title: Install the stretcher
steps.body: Run the stretcher between the two side supports with pocket holes and glue. This little H-brace is what lets the stool double as seating — don't skip it because the frame already feels stiff.

steps.title: Slat the top
steps.body:
Lay the five top slats with even gaps — the overhang past the aprons should match all around — and fasten from below through the aprons, or glue and brad from above if you don't mind tiny visible holes. Gaps drain rain; this is still outdoor furniture.

Sand everything, round the top edges and corners well (shins find end tables), and finish to match the chairs it will live between.

images.alt: Small square wood stool with slatted top, used as an outdoor end table
